channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Half of Gen Z Uses AI, but Their Feelings Are Souring, Study Shows
item.description: A new study from Gallup found that young adults have grown less hopeful and more angry about artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Callie Holtermann
item.media:credit: Andria Lo for The New York Times

item.title: Is the Strait of Hormuz Reopening? What to Know Under Iran-Cease Fire.
item.description: Vessels are wary of passing the coast of Iran in the strait, given the fragile agreement, and the number of ships traveling through it has even dropped.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels off Oman on Wednesday. Iran has said ships that want to pass must coordinate with its armed forces.

item.title: White House Secures Foreign Steel for Trump’s Ballroom Project
item.description: ArcelorMittal, a European steel maker, is donating tens of millions of dollars of foreign steel for President Trump’s new ballroom.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Luke Broadwater
item.media:description: The construction site for the new White House ballroom last month.

item.title: Fed Minutes Show Officials in No Rush to Cut as Iran War Scrambled Outlook
item.description: The conflict in the Middle East has left the Federal Reserve braced for higher inflation, with more officials open to the possibility of rate increases.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair.

item.title: Oil Prices Fall, but Energy Firms Remain Frozen After U.S.-Iran Deal
item.description: Analysts said oil and natural gas energy companies would not quickly restore production unless attacks stopped and ships started moving through the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Tankers anchored last month off Muscat, Oman, near the Strait of Hormuz. Until shipping traffic starts moving again, oil and gas companies are not going to reopen the taps.

item.title: Adam Back Denies He Is Satoshi Nakamoto in Response to Times Investigation
item.description: “Dr. Adam Back has consistently stated that he is not Satoshi Nakamoto,” his company said in a statement. “What is not speculative is Adam’s foundational contribution to Bitcoin.”
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: Adam Back onstage at a conference in Miami in February.

item.title: Amazon and U.S. Postal Service Reach New Deal on Deliveries After Year of Talks
item.description: Amazon will cut the volume of packages it ships through the Postal Service by 20 percent under a tentative new deal that will preserve a crucial source of income for the agency.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross and Aimee Ortiz
item.media:credit: Justin T. Gellerson for The New York Times
item.media:description: U.S.P.S. relies on Amazon for billions of dollars in revenue and has long struggled to stabilize its finances.

item.title: Why Gas Prices Won’t Fall as Quickly as Oil Prices
item.description: The energy industry refers to the behavior of gasoline prices as “up like a rocket, down like a feather.”
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: It could take time for U.S. gasoline prices to come down.

item.title: Jeff Shell Steps Down as President of Paramount
item.description: Mr. Shell, the former chief executive of NBCUniversal, is leaving after becoming entangled in a legal battle with a professional gambler, R.J. Cipriani.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Stella Kalinina for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paramount is trying to finalize a merger of roughly $111 billion with Warner Bros. Discovery, a deal that could reshape Hollywood.

item.title: Blue Collar Work Has Plateaued, Narrowing Options for Young Workers
item.description: Skilled electricians, plumbers and factory workers are in demand, but job openings have dropped.
item.dc:creator: Talmon Joseph Smith
item.media:credit: Amanda Lucier for The New York Times
item.media:description: Business for Electric Avenue has picked up in recent months.

item.title: Greece Plans to Block Social Media for Children Under 15
item.description: Countries including Australia, Spain and others have already passed bans or are working on plans to restrict teenagers’ access to social sites.
item.dc:creator: Niki Kitsantonis
item.media:description: Children checking their tablets and smartphones at a summer camp in Agios Andreas, Greece, last year.

item.title: ProPublica Journalists Strike for a Day, Partly Over A.I.
item.description: Other sticking points for journalists at the investigative nonprofit included demands for wage increases and layoff protections.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.media:credit: Jackie Molloy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Picketing outside ProPublica’s Manhattan office. About 150 ProPublica workers walked off the job on Wednesday.

item.title: After Iran Cease-Fire, Ship Traffic in Strait of Hormuz Remains Throttled
item.description: A handful of vessels have crossed the crucial waterway since the U.S.-Iran truce began, but shipowners, insurers and others are wary of safe passage.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis and John Yoon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Cargo ships near the Strait of Hormuz last month. Traffic through the strait has slowed to a trickle during the war.

item.title: Investors Still Have Questions About the Cease-Fire
item.description: Analysts and traders are anxiously watching the Strait of Hormuz and whether peace talks can resolve big differences between the United States and Iran.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly and Brian O’Keefe
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Stock markets worldwide jumped today after the U.S. and Iran reached a cease-fire.

item.title: Delta Expects Strong Profit Despite Higher Fuel Costs
item.description: The airline said demand for tickets remained very strong despite the effects of the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Delta Air Lines said it expected to earn a profit of about $1 billion, before taxes, in the quarter ending in June.

item.title: Gas Prices Won’t Quickly Return to Prewar Levels, Even if the Strait of Hormuz Reopens
item.description: Some wells can be turned on in days or weeks, but bringing the Gulf’s energy system back to something akin to normal will take months.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Ivan Penn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Unraveling the Mystery Behind Bitcoin’s Creator
item.description: Our investigative reporter John Carreyrou spent 18 months digging through the archives of online cryptography communities in search of the identity of Satoshi Nakamoto, the anonymous inventor of bitcoin.
item.dc:creator: John Carreyrou, Sutton Raphael, James Surdam, Coleman Lowndes and Joey Sendaydiego

item.title: Oil Prices Plunge and Stocks Surge After Cease-Fire Deal
item.description: Despite confusions about the terms of the deal, investors on Wednesday welcomed the move toward de-escalation between Iran and the United States.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Who Is Satoshi Nakamoto? My Quest to Unmask Bitcoin’s Creator
item.description: Bitcoin’s creator has hidden behind the pseudonym Satoshi Nakamoto for 17 years. But a trail of clues buried deep in crypto lore led to a 55-year-old computer scientist named Adam Back.
item.dc:creator: John Carreyrou and Dylan Freedman

item.title: 4 Takeaways From Our Search for Satoshi Nakamoto, Bitcoin’s Creator
item.description: Here’s what we found suggesting that Adam Back invented Bitcoin.
item.dc:creator: John Carreyrou and Dylan Freedman
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: Adam Back in Miami in February.

item.title: How China Built Its Vast Natural Gas Stockpile
item.description: Natural gas is hard to store, but China has found a way to do it, while also developing alternate suppliers and expanding production at home.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times
item.media:description: China’s natural gas stockpiles — the world’s largest above ground — helped the country cushion the shock from the war in the Middle East.

item.title: To Boost Military Budget, Trump Targets Popular Programs at Home
item.description: Amid the war with Iran, the president has proposed to scale back some of the very programs meant to ease families’ financial burdens.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Annie Karni
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: For President Trump, the renewed debate over federal spending arrives at a perilous political and economic moment.

item.title: Delta, Southwest, WestJet and Other Airlines Announce Higher Baggage Fees and Fuel Surcharges
item.description: American and Canadian airlines, squeezed by surging fuel costs from the war in Iran, are adding “sticky” new bag fees and surcharges, on top of higher fares.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Delta Air Lines is raising its checked-bag fee by $10, joining United Airlines and JetBlue Airways, which both raised their checked-bag fees last week.

item.title: Gulf Backers Invest $24 Billion in Paramount’s Deal for Warner Bros.
item.description: Despite speculation that the Iran war could scuttle commitments from Saudi Arabia and other Middle Eastern countries, their investments have been secured.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Benjamin Mullin
item.media:credit: Ricardo Nagaoka for The New York Times
item.media:description: Paramount beat out Netflix in February to secure a $110 billion deal to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery.

item.title: ‘Definitely a Sham’: As Tariffs Climb, Trade Fraud and Accounting Tricks Proliferate
item.description: U.S. imports from China have shrunk drastically. But billions of dollars of the change appear to be the result of accounting gimmicks and outright fraud.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times

item.title: ‘The Late Show With Stephen Colbert’ Being Replaced by ‘Comics Unleashed,’ CBS Says
item.description: The comedian and longtime producer will bring his syndicated programming to the late-night slot that “The Late Show With Stephen Colbert” will vacate in May.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Byron Allen in Los Angeles in 2021.

item.title: Despite High Oil Prices and Volatile Stocks, Companies See Openings for Deals
item.description: Bankers say companies are braving higher oil prices and whipsawing stock prices to seize on the willingness of federal antitrust enforcers to approve mergers.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Bryan Thomas for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Jetro Restaurant Depot outlet in the Bronx. Sysco’s bid to acquire Jetro for $29 billion will test regulators’ appetite for consolidation in the food industry.

item.title: Markets on Edge as Trump’s Iran Ultimatum Looms
item.description: Oil prices are rising and stocks are selling off as investors fret that the president will carry out his threats to “decimate” Iran.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Michelle Leder
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has rattled markets as he intensifies his threats against Iran.

item.title: Bill Ackman’s Pershing Square Bids to Buy Universal Music Group
item.description: The complex transaction pitched by the billionaire hedge fund manager would need to pass muster with the music giant’s shareholders.
item.dc:creator: Michael J. de la Merced and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Cassidy Araiza for The New York Times
item.media:description: Taylor Swift is a member of the Universal Music Group roster, along with Bad Bunny, Kendrick Lamar, Coldplay and others.

item.title: Justice Dept. Resolves Broadway Touring Company Investigation
item.description: Broadway Across America will not be prosecuted after acknowledging it signed a noncompete agreement with another presenter, the agency said.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch and Michael Paulson
item.media:description: The Majestic Theater in San Antonio in 2018. The Justice Department focused on an agreement between Broadway Across America and a company that managed shows in San Antonio.

item.title: Presto! In Chicago, a High-End Magic Palace Appears
item.description: The entrepreneur Glen Tullman is betting people want to dress up and watch magicians in a luxury setting. Either it will work or $50 million will go poof.
item.dc:creator: David Segal and Evan Jenkins
item.category: Hand and the Eye, The (Metamorphosis Chicago LLC)
item.media:description: Glen Tullman, the health care entrepreneur bankrolling The Hand and the Eye, has been a magic fan his entire life.

item.title: Tucson: A City for Outdoor Lovers
item.description: Owls, oases and the Milky Way reward adventure seekers in and around this Arizona city in the Sonoran Desert.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Glusac and Nate Abbott
item.media:description: Horse-backing riding is one of many outdoor activities in the Tucson area.

item.title: Finding Trends in Sports Stats and ‘Wheel of Fortune’ Puzzles
item.description: Ben Blatt, a data reporter for The Upshot, dreams up inventive ways to answer questions like: “Is Taylor Swift actually increasing N.F.L. ratings?”
item.dc:creator: Sarah Bahr

item.title: Oil and Stock Prices Reverse Some Moves as a Peace Deal Remains Uncertain
item.description: President Trump's Tuesday evening deadline for a deal approached after he rejected a cease-fire proposal as “not good enough.”
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil prices jumped on Tuesday after President Trump rejected a cease-fire proposal with Iran. U.S. gas prices rose to a national average of $4.14 a gallon.

item.title: Trump Threatens Jail if Journalists Protect Certain Iran Sources
item.description: The president indicated he would ask an unnamed media outlet to reveal the sources behind its coverage of Iran’s successful strike on a U.S. fighter jet, and of its crew.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump at a news conference in Washington on Monday.

item.title: BNY and Robinhood Will Help Run ‘Trump Accounts’ for Children
item.description: The new tax-sheltered savings and investment accounts will start accepting deposits this summer.
item.dc:creator: Stacy Cowley
item.category: Bank of New York Mellon Corporation
item.media:description: President Trump created the accounts as part of his signature domestic spending bill passed last year.

item.title: Do You Have Questions About a No-Bid Federal Contract? Tell Us Here.
item.description: The government is supposed to let many vendors compete for contracts, to get the best deal for taxpayers. We are looking at cases where it did not.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Andrea Fuller
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The White House this month. The New York Times is seeking information about no-bid contracts issued by the Trump administration.

item.title: A New Oil Shock Accelerates a Return to Nuclear Power
item.description: Shocks to natural gas supplies are spurring countries in Asia and elsewhere to rethink their rejection of nuclear energy after the 2011 disaster in Fukushima, Japan.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis and Meaghan Tobin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ko Sasaki for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Fukushima power plant, in 2019. An earthquake and tsunami triggered a triple meltdown 15 years ago, scaring many governments off nuclear energy.

item.title: Jamie Dimon Warns Investors Over Risks From War and Threat of Rising Inflation
item.description: In his annual letter to shareholders, Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JPMorgan Chase, said investors and businesses faced a series of challenges.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Grady McGregor
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Jamie Dimon, the C.E.O. of JPMorgan Chase, said the war in Iran could pose potentially big risks.

item.title: Savannah Guthrie Returns to ‘Today’
item.description: The anchor joined the show’s cast on Monday for the first time since the disappearance of her 84-year-old mother, Nancy, who remains missing.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin and Michael M. Grynbaum

item.title: What Does an Ultra-Luxury First Class Ticket Get You? Flying in a Frictionless Bubble.
item.description: A writer reports from inside the premium bubble, where there’s no such thing as too much, petty annoyances are nonexistent and the real world never intrudes.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall

item.title: Oil Seesaws After Trump’s Latest Threats on Iran
item.description: President Trump taunted Iranian leaders on Sunday, threatening to escalate attacks if the Strait of Hormuz was not reopened.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison and Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: China Mourned an Education Influencer. The Grief Was a Quiet Revolt.
item.description: Zhang Xuefeng helped people navigate the country’s unforgiving higher education system. The public outpouring after his death was a quiet rebuke to the punishing process.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan
item.media:description: Mourners lined up in Suzhou, China, to pay their respects to Zhang Xuefeng, a prominent education influencer who died on March 24.

item.title: China Started Preparing for an Energy Crisis Long Before the Iran War
item.description: Long concerned about geopolitical crises, China redoubled efforts to secure energy security when President Trump started raising the stakes in his first term.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil storage tanks and facilities of a Sinopec plant in Shanghai last month.

item.title: OPEC Plus Warns of Slow Recovery After War in Iran
item.description: The group of influential oil exporters also said it was increasing its production quotas, a largely symbolic move.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
item.media:description: Smoke rising from an oil warehouse following a suspected drone attack last week near Erbil, Iraq.

item.title: Despite Poor Reviews, ‘The Super Mario Galaxy Movie’ Is a Megahit
item.description: The animated sequel collected about $373 million worldwide over its first five days in theaters. “Kids love the movie,” a box office analyst said.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:description: “The Super Mario Galaxy Movie,” based on Nintendo characters, cost roughly $110 million to make.

item.title: New Chef Program Helps People With Autism Find Jobs in Fine-Dining Restaurants
item.description: Culinary jobs have the potential to be a perfect fit, and a new effort is afoot to help autistic workers land them.
item.dc:creator: Pete Wells
item.media:description: Joseph Valentino, left, a cook at Point Seven in Manhattan, and Franklin Becker, the restaurant’s owner. Mr. Valentino, who has autism, helped inspire a new program to place people on the autism spectrum in fine-dining jobs.

item.title: Hockey Rinks Turn to Plastic Ice as Planet Warms
item.description: Some environmentalists question using plastic to address rising temperatures.
item.dc:creator: Ken Belson and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Rebecca Gratz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Climate change has wreaked havoc with many sports. But few sports have been affected as much as those played on ice and snow.

item.title: New Owner of Shopping Plaza in Kansas City Pitches a $1.5 Billion Rescue Plan
item.description: The Country Club Plaza’s sale and turnaround plans come at a consequential economic time for Kansas City, Mo., which will soon lose the Chiefs football team.
item.dc:creator: Joe Gose

item.title: Oil Markets Doubt Trump’s Peace Signals as Tensions With Iran Grow
item.description: After seesawing between diplomacy and threats, the president may find it harder to knock down prices by suggesting pauses in attacks or progress in talks.
item.dc:creator: Amy Fan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
